title: API Standards Breakdown: API 598 vs 6A vs 6D - Know Your Standard
description: Your customer says "spec to API 6A." Your vendor says they comply with "API 598." Your inspector asks for "API 6D." Three different standards, three differen...
content:
The Three Standards and Why They Are Not Interchangeable

Your customer says "spec to API 6A." Your vendor says they comply with "API 598." Your inspector asks for "API 6D." Three different standards, three different expectations, and one very confused project team.

These are not interchangeable, and mixing them up can cause serious problems—from premature equipment failure to product liability to regulatory non-compliance.

Let's break down the major API standards and help you know which one applies to your project.

API 598: Valve Inspection and Testing

API 598 is the standard for inspection, testing, and certification of gates, globes, check, ball, and butterfly valves. It defines how valves are manufactured, tested, and verified to meet pressure ratings.

This standard applies to VALVES specifically—not to flanges, not to pressure vessels, but to the bolted valve bodies.

It requires that valves be hydrostatically tested to 1.5 times the rated pressure for a minimum of 5 seconds. It specifies which surfaces must have penetrant or magnetic particle inspection. It defines acceptable leakage rates through the valve seat and stem.

If you are specifying isolation valves or control valves for your pressure vessel, API 598 tells you how those valves should be tested and what the acceptable defect limits are.

API 6A: Wellhead Equipment for Oil and Gas

API 6A is the standard for subsurface and surface wellhead equipment in oil and gas production. It covers flanges, threaded connections, and integrated wellhead systems designed to handle extreme pressures, sour gas, and harsh operating environments.

This is NOT a general-purpose flange standard. It is specific to oil and gas wellhead service. A API 6A flange is typically thicker and stronger than an equivalent ASME B16.5 flange because wellheads experience higher pressures and must handle H2S (hydrogen sulfide) corrosion.

API 6A requires comprehensive material testing, surface finish requirements to prevent corrosion, and qualification testing for sour service. Components must be demonstrably fit for purpose in the worst-case scenario (maximum pressure + maximum temperature + maximum corrosion potential).

If your project is in offshore oil and gas, your customer will probably mandate API 6A. If it is in onshore petrochemical, you might be able to use ASME B16.5 instead (cheaper and often sufficient).

API 6D: Flanges for Pipeline and Refinery Service

API 6D covers flanges for integration into pipeline systems and refinery equipment. It is similar to ASME B16.5 but is specifically curated for oil and gas applications.

A 6D flange has slightly different pressure ratings and dimensional standards than a B16.5 flange of the same nominal size. Material specifications are tailored for sour service compatibility.

API 6D is less stringent than 6A. It is designed for production equipment and transmission lines, not for the extreme pressures of wellheads.

Choosing the Right Standard

Here is a simple framework:

ASME B16.5: General industrial use (water, inert gases, low-corrosion service). Most chemical plants and food processing use this. API 6D: Oil and gas pipeline or production equipment. Not as harsh as wellhead service but requires sour service compatibility. API 6A: Offshore oil and gas wellheads. Extreme pressure, extreme corrosion potential. Requires dense qualification and testing.

Do not use API 6A for general industrial service—it is overkill and will kill your budget. Do not use ASME B16.5 for sour oil service—it will corrode and fail prematurely.

Material Traceability and Certification

All three standards require material test reports (MTRs) and heat number traceability. But the depth of testing varies.

ASME B16.5 typically requires chemical composition and tensile test data. API 6D adds impact testing and metallurgical hardness verification. API 6A requires all of that plus fatigue testing and corrosion resistance testing for sour service.

The Bottom Line

Know your standard. Define it clearly in your purchase requisition. And do not let a vendor convince you that all standards are equivalent just because they sound similar. They are not. The time spent clarifying the right standard at the drawing stage will save months and thousands of dollars in rework and disputes later.
